children.name: Dicrurus
children.description: The typical drongos — a genus of medium-sized, entirely black or dark grey passerines with deeply forked tails, found across Africa, Asia, and Australasia. They are aggressive, fearless birds that will attack much larger species that approach their nests, including raptors and crows. They are accomplished aerial insectivores and noted vocal mimics, incorporating the calls of other birds into their own repertoire.

children.name: Dicrurus macrocercus
children.description: A glossy black drongo with a deeply forked tail, one of the most common and familiar birds of South and Southeast Asia, found in open country, farmland, and urban areas. It is an aggressive insectivore that hawks for prey from exposed perches and fearlessly mobs larger birds, including crows and raptors. Its wide distribution and adaptability have made it a familiar companion to livestock, catching insects disturbed by grazing animals.

children.name: Dicrurus leucophaeus
children.description: A medium-sized drongo with pale grey plumage, a distinctive red eye, and a deeply forked tail, found in forests and woodlands across South and Southeast Asia. Its plumage varies dramatically across its range, from near-white to slate-grey. It is an accomplished mimic that incorporates the calls of other birds into its own varied song.

children.name: Dicrurus bracteatus
children.description: A glossy black drongo with iridescent blue-green spangles on the breast, head, and wings, and a deeply forked, outward-curving tail, found from the Moluccas through New Guinea to eastern Australia. It is a bold, aggressive bird that harries larger species and is an expert aerial insect hunter. Its name has entered Australian slang to describe a foolish person.

children.name: Dicrurus paradiseus
children.description: A spectacular drongo with elongated outer tail feathers that end in broad, racket-like tips, found in forests from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Borneo. It is one of the finest avian mimics in Asia, perfectly imitating the calls of other birds, mammals, and even mechanical sounds. It is often a core member of mixed-species feeding flocks, acting as a sentinel for other birds.

children.name: Dicrurus annectans
children.description: A stocky drongo with a thick, crow-like bill, glossy black plumage, and a moderately forked tail, found from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. It is a forest-dwelling species that forages in the canopy, often joining mixed-species flocks. Its call is a harsh, grating note unlike the more musical calls of other drongos.

children.name: Dicrurus aeneus
children.description: A small, glossy drongo with a distinct bronze-green sheen on the upperparts and a moderately forked tail, found in forests from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Borneo. It is an active, restless forager that hawks insects from exposed perches in the mid-storey. Its song is a series of sweet, musical notes interspersed with imitations of other birds.

children.name: Dicrurus remifer
children.description: A sleek drongo with elongated outer tail feathers ending in narrow rackets, found in montane forests from the Himalayas through Southeast Asia to Sumatra and Java. It is smaller and more slender than the Greater Racket-tailed Drongo, with a less deeply forked tail. It is an accomplished mimic and a active forager in the forest canopy.

children.name: Dicrurus hottentottus
children.description: A large, glossy black drongo with a prominent tufted crest of fine, hair-like feathers on the forehead and a deeply forked tail, found from South Asia through Southeast Asia to the Philippines, Borneo, and Sulawesi. It is a bold and aggressive bird, often seen mobbing larger species. Its varied calls include harsh scolds and sweet, mimicking phrases.

children.name: Dicrurus balicassius
children.description: A medium-sized drongo endemic to the Philippines, with glossy black plumage and a long, deeply forked tail. It inhabits lowland and montane forests across most of the archipelago. Its name derives from the Spanish colonial period, and it is one of the most common forest drongos in the Philippines.

children.name: Dicrurus montanus
children.description: A dark drongo endemic to the mountain forests of Sulawesi, Indonesia, with entirely black plumage and a moderately forked tail. It is restricted to elevations above 1,000 metres and is one of the least studied drongos in Southeast Asia.

children.name: Dicrurus sumatranus
children.description: A little-known drongo endemic to the montane forests of Sumatra, Indonesia, with entirely black plumage and a forked tail. It is restricted to elevations above 800 metres on the Barisan mountain range and is among the rarest and most poorly documented drongo species.

children.name: Dicrurus densus
children.description: A medium-sized drongo found in the Wallacea region of Indonesia, including the Lesser Sundas and Moluccas, with glossy black plumage and a deeply forked tail. It inhabits lowland forests and secondary growth. Its distribution across the biogeographically transitional Wallacean islands makes it an important species for understanding drongo evolution.

children.name: Dicrurus andamanensis
children.description: A large, glossy black drongo endemic to the Andaman Islands, with a deeply forked tail and a distinctive greenish-blue gloss on the upperparts. It inhabits primary and secondary forests across the archipelago. Its loud, varied calls include excellent mimicry of other Andaman birds.
